content:
# Genesis Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 7v–8r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_013_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_013_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0012

---

## The Parting of Ways

**1** So Abram came up out of Egypt — he and his wife and everything he had, and Lot with him — and they traveled back to the Negev. **2** Abram was very wealthy: rich in livestock, silver, and gold.

**3** He continued on his journey from the Negev until he reached Bethel again — the place where he had camped between Bethel and Ai at the beginning, **4** the place where he had first built an altar. And there, once again, Abram called on the name of the LORD.

**5** Now Lot, who was traveling with Abram, also had flocks and herds and tents of his own. **6** The land simply could not support both of them together. Their possessions were too great for them to live side by side, **7** and quarreling broke out between Abram's herders and Lot's herders. (At that time, the Canaanites and Perizzites were also living in the land.)

**8** So Abram said to Lot, "Let there be no strife between you and me, or between your herders and mine — for we are family. **9** Is not the whole land open before you? Let us part ways. If you go left, I will go right; if you go right, I will go left."

**10** Lot looked up and saw the whole Jordan plain — how well-watered it was, everywhere, before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah. It was like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt, all the way to Zoar. **11** So Lot chose the whole Jordan plain for himself, and he set out to the east. That is how the two men parted ways. **12** Abram settled in the land of Canaan, while Lot settled among the cities of the plain and pitched his tents as far as Sodom. **13** Now the people of Sodom were deeply wicked — flagrant sinners against the LORD.

**14** After Lot had parted from him, the LORD said to Abram, "Lift up your eyes now, from where you are standing — look north, south, east, west. **15** All the land you can see, I will give to you and to your descendants forever. **16** I will make your descendants like the dust of the earth: if anyone could count the dust of the earth, then your descendants could be counted too. **17** Walk the length and breadth of the land, for I am giving it to you."

**18** So Abram moved his tents and came to live near the great oaks of Mamre at Hebron, and there he built an altar to the LORD.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 13 — after Egypt: what success costs

The chapter opens on the far side of disgrace. Abram has come up from Egypt — *alah*, the word used for ascending — with more wealth than he entered with and less innocence than he possessed before. The Egypt episode ended without resolution, without recorded repentance, without a divine word to Abram. He was expelled from Pharaoh's court, sent north with his wife and his livestock and his shame, and now he is retracing his steps back to Bethel, back to the altar he built, back to the place where he first called on the name of the LORD.

The detail that he returns to "the place where he had first built an altar" is not incidental. It is penitential geography. Abram is making his way back — physically, and perhaps spiritually — to the beginning of his life in the land, to the point before Egypt, before the fear, before the lie. He calls on the LORD's name again (v. 4). The narrative does not say what he prays. It simply notes that he called. The altar stands as it was. The LORD has not moved.

---

### Verses 2–7 — the problem of abundance

The irony of these verses is quiet but deliberate. The promise of Genesis 12 was abundance: a great nation, a great name, blessing. Abundance has arrived — but as a problem. Abram and Lot together have too much. The land cannot hold them. Their flocks are so large, their herders so numerous, that the finite territory around Bethel and Ai becomes a source of conflict rather than gift.

The narrator slips in a territorial note: "the Canaanites and Perizzites were also living in the land." This is the second time a version of this notice appears (cf. 12:6: "the Canaanites were in the land at that time"). Its function is to constrict the stage. The land is not empty. There is not unlimited room to expand in different directions. Abram and Lot are guests in a land already occupied, and their internal quarreling is happening within hearing of their neighbors. The honor of the household, and the promise tied to it, is at stake.

---

### Verses 8–9 — Abram's generosity: the faith move

Abram's offer to Lot is one of the most striking gestures in the patriarchal narrative — and it almost disappears in a plain reading. Read carefully, it is extraordinary.

Abram is the elder. In the world of this text, primogeniture and patriarchal seniority confer rights. Abram has the right to choose first. He is also — crucially — the one to whom the land has been *promised*. God has said to Abram: this land, to your descendants. Not Lot's descendants. Abram's. He has a divine deed in his hand, or rather in his memory, and yet he offers Lot the first choice: "Is not the whole land open before you? You choose. I'll take what's left."

The Hebrew of verse 9 is terse and beautiful: **הֲלֹא כָל-הָאָרֶץ לְפָנֶיךָ** — "Is not all the land before your face?" *Lefanekha* — before your face. The land is spread out in front of you. Look at it. Take what you want. The gesture is not merely generous; it is an act of faith. Abram is releasing a claim that was given to him, trusting that God will give it back. He does not need to grasp. He does not need to maneuver. He can afford to be generous because he is held by a promise larger than any visible territory.

The contrast with Lot will become the chapter's central meaning.

---

### Verse 10 — the fatal look: sight versus faith

**וַיִּשָּׂא לוֹט אֶת-עֵינָיו** — "Lot lifted up his eyes." The phrase is loaded. To lift the eyes in biblical narrative is a significant act; it often precedes either a divine encounter or a decisive choice. Here Lot lifts his eyes and sees — and what he sees is spectacular.

The Jordan plain (the *kikkar hayarden* — the "disk" or "circle" of the Jordan) is described in a double simile: "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt." Two comparisons, each freighted with a different kind of memory.

The "garden of the LORD" — *gan YHWH* — recalls Eden. The word *gan* is the very word used in Genesis 2 for the garden God planted at the beginning. Well-watered, lush, abundant: the Jordan plain looks like paradise. This is the first echo.

The second echo — "like the land of Egypt" — is the one that should give every careful reader pause. Egypt is the land from which Abram has just returned, the land of the famine response, the land of the lie, the land where Sarai was taken into Pharaoh's house, the land from which Abram was expelled. Egypt in this narrative is not a compliment. It is a warning. The Jordan plain is beautiful and fertile, yes — but it looks like Egypt. And Egypt, in the logic of the story being told, is the place of moral compromise, of slavery, of near-disaster.

Lot sees only the surface. He sees water. He sees green. He sees opportunity. He does not see Sodom.

The narrator makes sure we see it. The double simile is followed immediately by a parenthesis that crashes the beauty with violence: "before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah." This is proleptic — a narrative wink from the future. The plain looks like Eden. It looks like Egypt. But it will look like neither of those things for long, because God will destroy it. Lot is choosing paradise on borrowed time.

The Vaticanus LXX renders the double simile faithfully: **ὡς παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ὡσεὶ γῆ Αἰγύπτου** — "like the paradise of God and like the land of Egypt." The Greek *paradeisos* (from Persian *pairidaeza*, an enclosed garden) was already the word used for Eden in the LXX of Genesis 2–3. The Vaticanus preserves the Eden resonance, perhaps even heightens it for Greek readers who would have heard *paradeisos* as a word of royal luxury and enclosed beauty. Lot is choosing a garden. He does not know what grows in it.

---

### The Eden/Egypt/Sodom triangle

The three comparisons embedded in verse 10 form what is perhaps the chapter's most sophisticated piece of literary theology. Read in sequence, they chart a descent:

**Eden** — the original garden, the place of divine presence and human flourishing before the fall. Creation as God intended it.

**Egypt** — the land of the Nile, abundant and powerful, but the land of Pharaoh, the house of slavery, the place where Abram's faith faltered. Abundance without fidelity.

**Sodom** — the destination to which the Jordan plain is explicitly heading (v. 12: Lot "pitched his tents as far as Sodom"). The city of wickedness so extreme it will require divine destruction. Abundance without justice, without mercy, without any of the characteristics that make civilization human.

Lot's choice traces this descent. He looks and sees Eden. He settles toward Sodom. He never meant to go there. He chose water and grass. But sight-based choosing, the text implies, cannot see where it is going. Only the long perspective of promise — the kind of perspective that lets you offer your nephew first choice of land you have been promised — can navigate forward through time.

---

### Verse 13 — the verdict on Sodom

The narrator inserts a verdict before we have met Sodom's inhabitants: they were **רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד** — "evil and sinful against the LORD, greatly/exceedingly." The doubling of *ra'im* (evil) and *hatta'im* (sinners) piles the condemnation. The added **מְאֹד** (very much, exceedingly) removes any ambiguity. And the phrase "against the LORD" — *laYHWH* — is unusual. Sin is oriented. It is committed *against* someone. Sodom's sin is specifically directed: it violates the LORD himself. Lot has not noticed any of this. He has pitched his tents nearby, attracted by the scenery.

The Vaticanus LXX renders this as **πονηροὶ καὶ ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ σφόδρα** — "wicked and sinners before God, greatly." The same weight, the same intensifier, the same directional phrase: before God, in God's face, as it were. Sodom's wickedness is not merely social failure; it is theological confrontation.

---

### Verses 14–17 — the renewed promise: looking and receiving

After Lot departs, God speaks again. The speech is structured as the antithesis to Lot's sight-based choosing.

Lot *lifted his eyes and looked* — and chose. God now says to Abram: *lift your eyes and look* — north, south, east, west. The same phrase, the same action, but with a completely different orientation. Lot looked and took. God invites Abram to look and *receive*.

**שָׂא נָא עֵינֶיךָ** — "Lift up now your eyes." The particle *na* adds gentleness, almost an invitation: "Please, lift your eyes." "Now — look up." After Lot has gone with the best of the visible territory, God tells Abram to look at all of it: the whole horizon. Everything visible. Because all of it — including the ground Lot just took — is going to belong to Abram's descendants.

The promise now deepens. In 12:7 it was: "to your descendants I will give this land." Here it is: "all the land you can see, I will give to you *and to your descendants forever*" — **לְעֹלָם**. The time horizon stretches to eternity. And the imagery of verse 16 multiplies: "I will make your descendants like the dust of the earth." Dust — *afar* — is the most abundant substance imaginable, and also the humblest. It is what humans were made from (Genesis 2:7: "dust of the ground"). Abram's descendants will be as uncountable as the material of which every human being is made. The image is both cosmic and intimate.

Then God says: "Walk the length and breadth of the land, for I am giving it to you." *Walk through it.* Not survey it. Not purchase it. Not conquer it. *Walk it.* There is something almost ceremonial about this — the act of walking the land as a form of claiming, of becoming present to what has been given. Every footfall is an act of faith. Abram will walk a land he does not yet own, in confidence that it has been given.

---

### Verse 18 — the oaks of Mamre: the altar, again

The chapter ends as chapter 12 began: Abram building an altar. The location this time is the great oaks of Mamre, at Hebron. Hebron will become the most significant site in Abram's story — where he receives the three visitors, where he intercedes for Sodom, where he buries Sarah, where the patriarchs and matriarchs will be buried in the cave of Machpelah.

The great oaks (*eloney Mamre*) suggest a sacred site, a place of ancient numinous character. Abram does not build a city. He does not fortify a position. He plants no permanent structure except an altar. He is still moving, still tent-dwelling, still a sojourner. But he builds an altar — again.

The altar is Abram's signature. Every major moment in his story in Canaan is marked by one. They are the material residue of his relationship with God, the physical marks of divine encounter left in the landscape. Long after Abram himself is gone, the altars will remain — stones stacked in the ancient way, telling anyone who knows how to read them: *the LORD was here. A man stood here and called on his name. He did not own this land. He worshiped here anyway.*

---

### The structural irony of the chapter

Genesis 13 is built on a single ironic reversal. Lot makes the strategic choice and Abram makes the faithful choice — and by the end of the Lot narrative, we will understand that the strategic choice was catastrophic and the faithful choice was life.

Lot chose by sight: the best-looking land. He ended up in Sodom, escaped with nothing, lost his wife, and his lineage continues only through incestuous children born from desperate circumstances (Genesis 19). His story is a tragedy of excellent visibility. He saw exactly what was there — and he did not see what was coming.

Abram chose by promise: he let Lot take what he wanted, then received from God a promise larger than anything Lot had chosen. He received not the well-watered plain but the word of the LORD, not the view from the hillside but the inheritance of faith. His story is a comedy — in the classical sense — of absurd abundance given to someone who stopped grasping.

The text does not moralize. It simply shows. The contrast between the two men's postures — Lot looking and calculating, Abram offering and receiving — is laid out without commentary, and the subsequent chapters will do the work of explication that no commentary could improve upon.

---

### The double simile and the literary memory of the text

One more thought on verse 10: the Jordan plain is "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt." What does it mean for a stretch of ground to carry two incompatible memories simultaneously?

The garden of the LORD is the lost paradise — the place of original wholeness, before exile, before death. To say the plain looks like it is to awaken the oldest and deepest human longing: to go home, to return to the beginning, to find a land where things are as they should be.

The land of Egypt is the opposite: the place of exile, of slavery, of having to survive through deception. Egypt in Genesis is the negative pole of the world. It is what happens when you leave the promised land under pressure.

That the Jordan plain "looks like" both simultaneously is a profound observation about the nature of visible beauty. What the eyes see does not tell you which of these two things you are actually entering. The landscape cannot distinguish between paradise and its imitation. Only the word of God — the promise, the covenant, the long story of which this moment is a part — can make that distinction. Lot trusts his eyes. His eyes cannot read the future. They cannot read the parenthesis the narrator has inserted: "before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah."

You need the narrator for that. You need the word that stands outside the scene and reads it whole. That is what faith is, in the vocabulary of this text: living by the word that stands outside the visible moment and reads it whole.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 7v–8r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation marks. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 8–9 (pa_0008, pa_0009), with the chapter heading CAP. XIII visible at the top of pa_0008 and continuing into pa_0009.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
